life_stories.title: Plant closure in 2009
life_stories.event: Terry was working at a small auto parts manufacturer when the plant closed in the recession. He was given two weeks notice and a severance package that was technically legal and felt like a punishment for years of reliable work. His supervisor — who was also laid off — gave him a personal reference and helped him get the warehouse job. He has worked at the same distribution company for fifteen years now and has moved from floor associate to supervisor. The experience left him with a lasting distrust of large business decisions made by people who don't know his name.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Distinguishes trust in the people he works with directly (high) from trust in corporate decision-makers at distance (low). Business trust is relational and proximate — not categorical.

life_stories.title: VA healthcare — positive experience
life_stories.event: Terry's father is a Vietnam veteran who uses the VA healthcare system. When his father was diagnosed with early-stage prostate cancer five years ago, the VA oncology team provided care that Terry describes as the best healthcare his family has ever received. His father is in remission. The appointments were kept, the communication was clear, and no bill was ever sent. He is aware the VA has a bad reputation nationally. His family's experience was the opposite.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Government as an institution is not uniformly distrusted. The VA experience created a specific positive anchor that moderates his general government scepticism. He says 'it depends on the people and the programme.'

life_stories.title: Getting the supervisor promotion
life_stories.event: When the supervisor position opened up, Terry applied and was passed over for an external hire who lasted eight months. He reapplied, got the job, and was told by his regional manager that the first hire had been a mistake they had made before. He accepted that. He didn't love the process. But the company corrected it, acknowledged the error, and gave him the role with a meaningful pay increase. He has managed the same team for six years.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Employer trust is grounded in this specific moment — the company corrected a mistake and acknowledged it. His trust is not unconditional but it is genuine. He gives his employer benefit of the doubt in ambiguous situations.

coping_mechanism.description: Trusts what has worked before and the people directly around him. Extends institutional trust proximate to personal experience; withholds it from distant corporate or political actors.

key_tensions: Business is his primary employer and bread-winner category but corporate decision-makers at distance are distrusted
VA positive experience moderates general government distrust — his ranking is not simply 'all institutions distrusted'

narrative: I've been doing warehousing for twenty years, fifteen at this company. I know my job, my team trusts me, and I trust them. That's the thing about trust for me — it's about people I can see, not organisations I'm told to believe in. When the plant I worked at before closed, they sent me a letter with two weeks severance and that was that. Five hundred jobs, gone, and nobody who made that decision knew any of our names. So I don't have a lot of trust in corporations as, like, abstract things. The people I work with directly, different story. My dad gets his healthcare from the VA. I know the VA has a bad reputation from the news, but I've been in that waiting room, I've talked to his doctors. When he got his cancer diagnosis, those doctors were as good as anything I've seen anywhere. So I don't just say 'government is all broken' because my actual experience with one government programme says otherwise. Media I barely trust at all. They're all selling something. Scientists I trust more than politicians. CEOs of major companies, I honestly couldn't care less what they say publicly — they're managing their image, not telling me something true.
decision_bullets: Does not rank all institutions uniformly low — VA positive experience creates specific government trust exception.
Business at-distance distrusted; direct employer trusted based on track record of acknowledging mistakes.
Media rated as lowest trust category — perceives all outlets as selling a narrative.
CEO public statements discounted heavily — seen as image management rather than information.
Expert credibility moderate — not dismissive but does not automatically defer to credentials.
Peer and co-worker opinion is the primary trust input for decisions in his world.
Social media consumed for news but with explicit awareness it's not fully reliable — uses it anyway.
Government trust is mixed rather than uniformly low — personal positive experience moderates population-level scepticism.

core_memory.identity_statement: Warehouse supervisor who trusts people he can see, distrusts corporate decisions made at distance; VA experience moderates his government distrust.

life_defining_events.event: Plant closure — two weeks notice, no acknowledgment from anyone who knew his name
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Corporate distance = low trust; direct employer relationship = conditional trust

life_defining_events.event: Father's VA cancer treatment — positive, high-quality experience
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Government not uniformly distrusted; specific positive programme anchor moderates general scepticism
